items.question: Who uses k-Nearest Neighbors in practice?
items.answer: ML engineers and data scientists on tabular and embedding tasks, recommender teams for neighborhood-based scoring, and researchers as a nonparametric baseline when labeled exemplars are available and inference latency allows neighbor search.

items.question: What is the k-NN prediction rule?
items.answer: Store all training (xᵢ, yᵢ). For query x, compute distance to every training point, take the k smallest, then classify by majority neighbor label or regress by averaging neighbor targets (optionally distance-weighted).

items.question: When should you choose k-NN over a linear model?
items.answer: When decision boundaries are irregular in feature space, you have moderate n and low-to-moderate d, interpretability via similar examples matters, and you can afford per-query neighbor search after proper feature scaling.

items.question: Where does computation happen in the k-NN pipeline?
items.answer: Training only stores data; almost all work is at inference — computing distances (or querying an index) to find k neighbors, then aggregating their labels or targets for the final prediction.

items.question: Why does feature scaling matter for k-NN?
items.answer: Distance combines all feature dimensions; unscaled columns with large numeric range dominate L₂ or L₁ distance, so neighbors match on magnitude artifacts instead of meaningful similarity — see Scaling and Normalization.

items.question: How do you pick hyperparameter k?
items.answer: Sweep candidate k values on validation data (often odd k for binary classification), plot error vs k, and choose the value balancing bias and variance — small k fits local noise, large k oversmooths; see Model Complexity.

items.question: k-NN vs Support Vector Machines?
items.answer: k-NN is lazy: stores all data, decides locally by k neighbors at query time, O(n) scan per query. SVM is eager: learns a sparse boundary from support vectors during training, fixed-cost prediction. Both handle nonlinear structure but differ on memory, latency, and interpretability.

items.question: Small k vs large k in k-NN?
items.answer: Small k tracks local clusters tightly — low bias, high variance, sensitive to label noise. Large k averages over wider regions — smoother boundary, higher bias, lower variance. Validation picks the k that minimizes held-out error for your dataset.

items.question: If k-NN stores every training point, what does "training" actually learn?
items.answer: Nothing parametric — it memorizes the labeled dataset. Generalization comes from the assumption that nearby points in feature space share similar labels or targets, not from fitted weights.

items.question: Why might validation accuracy drop when k = 1 works best on training data?
items.answer: k = 1 memorizes each training point including noise; neighbors on validation points are different rows, so single-neighbor votes overfit idiosyncratic labels instead of stable local structure.

items.question: What happens to neighbor rankings if one feature is unscaled and 1000× larger than others?
items.answer: Distance is dominated by that feature's squared differences; two points can be "close" only because that column matches, even when other features differ — scaling fixes the geometry of neighbor search.

items.question: Cause: increase k from 3 to 51 on the same scaled dataset. Effect?
items.answer: Decision regions smooth out; validation bias typically rises and variance falls until error bottoms out then rises again if k is too large and washes out local class structure.

items.question: Cause: add 50 irrelevant random features without scaling or selection. Effect?
items.answer: Distances in high dimensions concentrate; neighbor sets become less informative and k-NN accuracy often degrades — curse of dimensionality dilutes signal from useful features.

items.question: What if k equals the full training set size n?
items.answer: Every query uses all points: classification collapses to the global majority class; regression predicts the global mean target — a constant baseline regardless of x.

items.question: What breaks k-NN at production scale without an index?
items.answer: Linear O(n·d) distance scan per query: latency and cost explode as n grows into millions, even though training stayed trivial — exact k-NN needs spatial indexes or approximate NN search.

items.question: Central concept: k-Nearest Neighbors
items.answer: Instance-based supervised learner: predict from k closest stored training examples using distance in feature space.

items.question: Branch: classification aggregation
items.answer: Majority vote among k neighbor labels; handle ties with odd k, weighted vote, or smallest-distance tie-break.

items.question: Branch: regression aggregation
items.answer: Mean or distance-weighted mean of k neighbor target values y — same neighbor search, different output rule.

items.question: Branch: prerequisites and tuning
items.answer: Scale features (Scaling and Normalization), choose metric (Distance Metrics), tune k for bias–variance (Model Complexity).
